items.text: I give to my sister Joan... the sum of twenty pounds and all my wearing apparel and the house wherein she now dwelleth during her natural life
items.context: Bequest to his sister Joan Hart in Shakespeare's will, providing both money, personal items, and housing security
items.publicReaction: The bequest secured his sister's financial security and demonstrates family care in Shakespeare's estate planning
items.source: Shakespeare's Last Will and Testament, National Archives and Shakespeare Documented

items.text: I give my sword to Mr Thomas Combe
items.context: Personal bequest in Shakespeare's will of his sword to Thomas Combe of Warwickshire
items.publicReaction: This specific bequest of a personal item suggests a valued relationship or particular respect for Combe
items.source: Shakespeare's Last Will and Testament, National Archives and Shakespeare Documented

items.text: my broad silver gilt bowl to my daughter Judith
items.context: Specific bequest in Shakespeare's will of a valuable silver bowl to his daughter Judith
items.publicReaction: The bequest of this precious object alongside financial settlements provided Judith with both movable wealth and heirlooms
items.source: Shakespeare's Last Will and Testament, National Archives and Shakespeare Documented

items.text: TO THE ONLIE BEGETTER OF THESE INSUING SONNETS MR W H ALL HAPPINESSE AND THAT ETERNITIE PROMISED BY OUR EVER-LIVING POET WISHETH THE WELL-WISHING ADVENTURER IN SETTING FORTH T T
items.context: Dedication by Thomas Thorpe in the 1609 first edition of Shakespeare's sonnets, addressing the mysterious 'Mr W.H.' and crediting Shakespeare as the 'ever-living poet'
items.publicReaction: The mysterious dedication sparked centuries of scholarly debate about whether Shakespeare authorized publication and who 'Mr. W.H.' was
items.source: Shakespeare's Sonnets, first edition published by Thomas Thorpe, Shakespeare Documented
